$DCTH Some information was provided in the last call about the farthest advanced of the Phase 2 trials, which addresses mCRC liver cancer dominant. The aim is to activate all 26 of the planned trial sites by the end of 2026. 13 sites are currently active with a total of seven patients. There was also a mention of interim data from this trial at some point next year.

$DCTH The current target for sites approved for Hepzato in uveal melanoma is 37 in 2026, and 40 by Q1 of 2027. My impression from the last call was that that number is likely to increase. CEO Michel said that he had talked with as many as 48 institutions, and that there was tremendous interest. Right now there are 31 sites.
$DCTH the addition of Sutter Health is pretty significant. They were listed on the HepzatoKit website in January 2025 and only treated their first patient in July 2026 - 18 months.

JPMorgan🏁 $LRMR and said: We are initiating coverage of Larimar Therapeutics (LRMR) with an Overweight rating and a Dec 2027 price target of $9. 
$PTCT SLDB BIIB DSGN $LXEO 
Larimar's lead asset is nomlabofusp (nomla; CTI-1601), a subcutaneously administered, recombinant fusion protein designed to deliver frataxin (FXN) to the mitochondria of patients with Friedreich's ataxia (FA), a rare neurodegenerative disorder. 
 
In the near-term, in 2Q26, we expect to see topline results from the ongoing open-label (OL) study as well as feedback from the company's type B meeting with the FDA to align on the overall BLA package readiness. 
 
The company expects a rolling BLA submission for accelerated approval (AA) in June 2026, pending FDA feedback, using skin FXN levels as a surrogate endpoint, with the final submission including the CMC module expected in 2H26; if approved, nomla would be the first FXN replacement therapy for FA, with a potential U.S. approval and launch in 1H27.

NVO.body: $NVO Good for Lilly = bad or irrelevant for Novo. ..

$NVO $LLY Novo Nordisk Sues Eli Lilly Over Weight-Loss Drug Advertising 
 
Novo a little Jelly? 
 
Novo Nordisk has filed a lawsuit accusing Eli Lilly of misleading consumers through advertising for its GLP-1 weight-loss drugs. Novo alleges Lilly used outdated studies and compared higher-dose Lilly treatments against lower-dose Novo therapies, with dosage differences disclosed only in fine print. The legal battle marks another escalation in the competition between the two companies, which dominate the rapidly growing GLP-1 market.

$LLY $PFE $NVO $HIMS Trump Announces Phased Tariffs On Generic Drug Imports To Boost U.S. Manufacturing 
 
President Trump announced a phased tariff plan targeting imported generic drugs, with 0% tariffs through August 2028, followed by a 100% tariff beginning in August 2028 and increasing to 200% in August 2029. The administration said the goal is to encourage generic drug manufacturers to shift production to the United States. Companies participating in the administration's "most favored nation" drug pricing agreements will be exempt from tariffs for three years, while major suppliers in India and manufacturers relying on Chinese active pharmaceutical ingredients could face significant impacts.

RPID.body:
$RPID On the face of it, I love RPID's automation idea.   However, after some thought and research following the last presentation with Amgen, I see issues with this type of disruptive technology. 
1) RPID's special plates claim to have a longer dessication profile, which for the most part has been countered with the big microbiology companies offering extra-filled agar plates, which takes away from their savings equation. 
2) The time to implementation is longer than I expected (in years), giving other automation companies runway to improve microbiology operations with less disruption. 
3) Relationships with existing vendors is a large barrier in the microbiology world.  (Specifically calling out the inertia within microbiology) 
4) What happen's if RPID cannot supply their proprietary plates?   Can labs that transition fully to RPID transition back to normal plates and not disrupt plant operations? (Risk)

$ABVX “Actually, the data shows the opposite on induction and a very strong overall setup for Obe:
Induction (ABTECT-1/2, 8 weeks, refractory patients):
•  Obe 50 mg: ~20-22% clinical remission (placebo-adjusted ~13-19%)
•  Obe 25 mg: similar/strong in one study (~24%) This is solid for a novel miR-124 mechanism in a tough population (many failed advanced therapies). Cross-trial: comparable or competitive with other orals in similar settings. ￼
Maintenance (ABTECT, Week 44, induction responders re-randomized):
•  50.8% (25 mg) and 51.3% (50 mg) clinical remission vs 10.4% placebo
•  Placebo-adjusted Δ39.3% / Δ40.3% (p<0.0001) — among the best reported in Phase 3 UC maintenance (beats or matches Rinvoq’s ~39% adjusted in comparable analyses; lowest placebo rate seen).
•  All key secondaries met (endoscopic improvement/remission, HEMI, steroid-free, sustained remission). ￼
The maintenance pool isn’t “narrow”—high durability + dose escalation data (Part 2: strong recapture in relapsers/non-responders) supports broad/long-term use. Favorable safety (no new signals; malignancies in line with UC background rates in pooled data). Oral, once-daily convenience is a big plus.

$ABVX   
Rinvoq’s induction looks clearly stronger here, around 22% vs 11.4% for Obe.   
That leaves Obe’s maintenance pool constrained to a much narrower responder subset.   
Weak induction can cap the long-term setup.

$ABVX chart definitely looks interesting, but a clean setup alone doesn’t guarantee a winning trade. 
 
One thing I’ve learned over time: the market environment usually matters more than the pattern itself. 
 
A perfect chart fighting against weak sector momentum or a bad market backdrop can fail quickly. Meanwhile, even a decent setup in a strong environment can turn into a powerful move. 
 
The edge isn’t just finding the right chart. It’s understanding the bigger picture, trend, liquidity, and market conditions. 
 
Watching $ABVX, but keeping the broader environment in mind.
